Victorville has long been a stopping off point for Las Vegas bound travelers.
And just beyond the acres of new housing developments and chain restaurants, you'll find yourself in old town on Route 66.
We're really quite a little town, old town atmosphere, before they get into the glamour and the glitz - let's face it.
Sharon Foster is longtime resident and volunteer at Victorville's Route 66 Museum.
She says she's thought quite a bit about the proposed bullet train that would whiz tourists from her city to Las Vegas.
I think there is a need.
I know we've talked about it how many years?
But I think its time has come.
(SOUNDBITE OF ROADWAY)
Thirty miles up the road, you hit Barstow.
It's also a popular stop for tourists.
Barstow's city manager Curt Mitchell says the train will go through the town.
But it's going to go fast.
(SOUNDBITE OF LAUGHTER)
So there won't be a stop in Barstow.
It's just going to go right through.
Mitchell says about a year ago city leaders decided to oppose the high-speed rail after a study determined their city stood to lose more than 2,300 jobs.
Today, on the hope that some construction jobs will come their way, city hall is taking a different tone.
We're assuming its going forward and we're trying to do everything we can to work with the DesertXpress folks to make sure this as much a positive thing as we can.
Down on Barstow's Main Street, restaurant owner Maxim Atalla is not having any of it.
A project like that could just ruin everything for me.
He says along this stretch there are dozens of motels restaurants and gas stations that rely on Vegas traffic.
Here, if you fill up here, you get something to eat; you can make the two-hour drive no problems.
And that brings us to the question most often raised about the train - think math story problem.
From Victorville, driving at the posted speed of 70 miles an hour, how long would it take to go the remaining 187 miles to Las Vegas?
The answer, just over two and half hours.
I asked traveler Roger Rivera if he would consider parking his car to board a train that would shave a little more than an hour off his drive time.
It would depend on the price.
But driving up to Victorville, I'm half-way there from where I'm at to get there.
So it wouldn't be much farther.
Once you're there in Vegas, I mean where is it going to drop you off?
Christine Torres was driving from Las Vegas back to Southern California.
Because, you know, transportation can get kind of pretty pricey out there to your hotels.
For most of the trip from Los Angeles to Victorville and then onto Barstow, you navigate heavy traffic, industry and billboards.
When you get to Barstow, the scenery changes, says resident Carol Randall.
When I ask about the bullet train, she shakes her head.
At 150 miles an hour she says travelers will not only miss her town, but some of the best part of the Mojave Desert.
The colors on the mountains or the spring flowers that are out, and the color that is out there.
And there's a lot to see.
If federal funding is approved, the project could break ground early next year.

In 1992, this song launched the careers of the Minnesota band The Jayhawks.
They quickly became seminal forces in the burgeoning sound known as alternative country.
Co-founders Mark Olson and Gary Louris found their harmonies and their songwriting styles fit together like few others: a perfect match.
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""WAITING FOR THE SUN"")
(Singing) It was not lost on me.
But by the end of 1995, Mark Olson left the band.
Gary Louris kept the name and the spirit alive with three more Jayhawks albums without Olson, but it never quite sounded like this.

When I spoke with them yesterday, Mark told me he left the band not because of any animosity but just to find out what else he could do with his life.

And that only went on for really seven, eight years, and then Gary and I got back together and started playing music again.

Despite that threat, about 30 sellers have come forward to pay their taxes.

But to address the remaining pot clubs' worries over privacy and prosecution, the Board of Equalization has revised its policies, allowing merchants to get a seller's permit without revealing what they sell.
Board member Betty Yee says that should allay the club owner's fears of being busted by the feds after paying their taxes.

The only information that we would ask of them is the name of the business, their location, Social Security numbers, bank accounts, anything that's required for how we would process their collection and remittance of sales tax.
The other states allow patients or their caregivers to grow or possess small quantities of marijuana, but not to buy it.
Tax official Yee feels charging sales tax will help give medical marijuana dispensaries something they say they've always wanted - legitimacy.
It really is about how to be a credible establishment doing business in a community and being a fair and honest taxpayer.
